title: Eliminate Low Value

content.statement: If removing something does not reduce the value of the business, it should probably be removed, delayed, or simplified.
content.question: What is the one thing, if you eliminated this, would no longer be valuable to you?
content.equation: Business Leverage = Value Created ÷ Complexity Carried
content.mental_model: Emptying a backpack, if it does not help the climb, it is weight.
content.business_lens: Use this to cut features, tools, campaigns, meetings, offers, or workflows that do not increase customer value, revenue, retention, or trust.
content.life_lens: Stop carrying responsibilities, habits, or plans that no longer make your life meaningfully better.
sop: List the thing you are considering keeping
Ask the person: 'What is the one thing, if I eliminated this, would no longer be valuable to you?'
Identify which parts are essential versus optional
Remove, delay, or simplify what does not protect value
Reinvest the freed energy into the parts people actually care about
content.explainer: Eliminate Low Value is the discipline of testing whether something actually matters. Many businesses keep things because they already built them, because someone requested them once, or because removing them feels scary. This question asks the person to identify the piece that carries the value. If removing something would not change their desire, trust, retention, or willingness to pay, it may not deserve space. Every extra thing adds maintenance, confusion, and decision load. This principle protects simplicity by making every piece defend its value.
questions: What is the one thing, if you eliminated this, would no longer be valuable to you?
What have you kept building or maintaining purely because removing it would feel embarrassing, not because it earns its keep?
If you deleted your least-used feature, offer, or meeting tomorrow, who would actually notice, and would they be upset, or relieved?
content.equation_example: Rate each 1-10. Value Created 4 (low), Complexity Carried 8 (high, lots of maintenance, confusion, decision load). Business Leverage = 4 divided by 8 = 0.5, below 1, meaning this thing is costing more than it's worth. A high-value, low-complexity item scores well above 1, meaning it's earning its place. Above 1 means keep it; well below 1 means cut, delay, or simplify it.
content.closing_statement: Everything you keep should have to defend its complexity.

caption:
Principle Nº 243: Eliminate Low Value

If removing something does not reduce the value of the business, it should probably be removed, delayed, or simplified.

Ask yourself: What is the one thing, if you eliminated this, would no longer be valuable to you?
